exercises.title: Work-Life Balance Audit
exercises.description: For one week, track: (1) when you start and stop working, (2) how many times you check work after hours, (3) weekend work hours, (4) end-of-day energy level (1-10). Then analyze: where are your biggest boundary violations?
hints: Be honest — 'I checked Slack once' counts as a violation
Use phone screen time data to verify your self-report
Look for patterns: certain days or situations trigger boundary violations

solution.code:
WORK-LIFE BALANCE AUDIT:

| Day | Start | Stop  | After-hours checks | Weekend work | Energy |
|-----|-------|-------|-------------------|--------------|--------|
| Mon | 8:45  | 5:30  | 2 (Slack at 7 PM) | -            | 5/10   |
| Tue | 8:30  | 6:15  | 3 (email, Slack)   | -            | 4/10   |
| Wed | 8:50  | 5:45  | 1 (Slack)          | -            | 5/10   |
| Thu | 8:40  | 5:30  | 0                  | -            | 6/10   |
| Fri | 8:45  | 5:15  | 0                  | -            | 6/10   |
| Sat | -     | -     | 1 (email)          | 2 hours      | 4/10   |
| Sun | -     | -     | 0                  | 1 hour       | 5/10   |

ANALYSIS:
• Average after-hours checks: 1/day (weekdays), 0.5/day (weekend)
• Weekend work: 3 hours total
• Average energy: 5.0/10
• Biggest violations: Tuesday (3 checks), Saturday (2 hours work)
• Pattern: Stressful deadlines trigger after-hours checking

PRIORITY FIXES:
1. Delete Slack from personal phone (eliminates after-hours checks)
2. No weekend work unless production emergency
3. Communicate 9-5:30 boundary to team

exercises.title: Boundary Communication Script
exercises.description: Write and deliver a message to your team/manager communicating one new boundary. Use a framing that connects the boundary to work quality, not personal preference. Practice the conversation beforehand and note how people respond.
hints: Frame as 'I'll produce better work if...' not 'I don't want to...'
Be specific about the boundary (times, channels, exceptions)
Expect some pushback initially — consistency wins over time

solution.code:
BOUNDARY COMMUNICATION SCRIPTS:

TO MANAGER:
'Hey [name], I've been thinking about how to do my best work. I've noticed
I'm more focused and creative when I have uninterrupted morning blocks.
Would it be okay if I protect 9-11 AM as deep work time? I'll be
available for meetings and Slack after 11 AM. I think this will help
me deliver higher-quality code on the projects I'm working on.'

TO TEAM (Slack message):
'Hi team — I'm setting up some focus time to improve my output. I'll be
in deep work mode 9-11 AM on weekdays. I'll check Slack at 11 AM and
be fully available after that. If something is truly urgent during
those hours, feel free to call me. Otherwise, I'll respond at 11.'

TO FAMILY:
'I'm going to start disconnecting from work at 5:30 PM every day. That
means no checking email or Slack after that time. If you notice me
checking my phone after 5:30, please remind me — it helps me stick
to the boundary.'

EXPECTED RESPONSES:
• Manager: Usually supportive (better output = better for them)
• Team: May need 1-2 weeks to adjust, then they respect it
• Family: Usually relieved (they've been wanting you to disconnect)

exercises.title: Design Your Disconnection Protocol
exercises.description: Create a detailed end-of-day disconnection protocol that includes: a shutdown ritual, a transition activity, and rules for after-hours communication. Practice for 2 weeks and track how your evenings and sleep quality change.
hints: The shutdown ritual should be specific and consistent (same steps, same time)
The transition activity should be physically different from work (change rooms, change clothes)
Write the rules down and share them with your household for accountability

solution.code:
DISCONNECTION PROTOCOL:

SHUTDOWN RITUAL (5:15-5:30 PM daily):
1. Review task list, check off completed items
2. Quick email/Slack scan (5 min max)
3. Write tomorrow's 3 priorities
4. Close all work applications
5. Say out loud: 'Shutdown complete'

TRANSITION ACTIVITY (5:30-6:00 PM):
• Change out of work clothes
• Walk around the block (10 min)
• OR: Cook dinner (hands-on activity)
• OR: Exercise (30 min workout)

AFTER-HOURS RULES:
1. No Slack/email on personal phone (apps deleted)
2. Work phone in drawer from 5:30 PM
3. If truly urgent (production down), team will call
4. No 'just checking one thing' — ever
5. Weekend rule: no work unless production emergency

HOUSEHOLD COMMUNICATION:
'Hey, I'm going to be completely offline from 5:30 PM onward.
If you see me checking my phone after that, please remind me.
I'm doing this to be more present at home and sleep better.'

TRACKING (2 weeks):
| Week | Shutdown done | After-hours checks | Sleep quality | Evening mood |
|------|--------------|-------------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1    | 4/5 weekdays | 1 (Wed: had to)   | 7/10          | 7/10        |
| 2    | 5/5 weekdays | 0                 | 8/10          | 8/10        |

FINDINGS:
• Sleep improved noticeably (less rumination)
• Evenings feel longer and more restful
• Work anxiety decreased (trust the system)
• Colleagues adjusted quickly to new availability
